title: Projections
learningObjectives: Derive the formula for orthogonal projection of a vector onto a direction
Compute projections numerically for specific vectors and directions
Define and compute the projection residual (error) and explain what it represents geometrically
Explain why projection is the fundamental operation of both dimensionality reduction and reconstruction
Relate the squared projection length to variance along a direction

whyThisLesson.text: PCA, SAEs, and all linear compression methods reduce to one operation: projection. When we say 'the first principal component explains 60% of the variance', we are saying that the sum of squared projections of all data points onto PC1 accounts for 60% of the total variation. When we say an SAE 'reconstructs the input with 95% accuracy', we mean the projection of the input onto the SAE's feature directions recovers 95% of the input's magnitude. Projection is the kernel of everything that follows. This lesson derives the formula from geometric first principles so that the mathematics is not mysterious.

sections.bridgeFrom: We understand that changing basis is a lossless rotation. But practical dimensionality reduction is LOSSY — we keep only some basis directions and discard others. The operation of 'keeping one component' is exactly projection. This lesson derives the projection formula.

intuition.analogy: Imagine a 3D object — a tilted rod — and you want to photograph it. The photograph is a 2D projection of the 3D object. From certain angles, you see most of the rod's length (the shadow is long). From others, you see almost nothing (the shadow is a dot). The orthogonal projection is the 'most faithful' capture of the rod's length in a given direction: it is the length of the shadow when the light is perpendicular to the projection plane. What you LOSE (the remaining depth you cannot see from that angle) is the residual — and it is always perpendicular to the direction you chose.
intuition.analogyLimit: A photograph projection is a 3D→2D operation and is visually intuitive. In n-dimensional spaces, projection is onto a 1D, 2D, or k-dimensional subspace. The perpendicularity of the residual holds in all dimensions, but there are infinitely many possible projection directions in high dimensions, making the analogy insufficient for guiding intuition about which directions are best.
intuition.text: The projection formula has a beautiful geometric interpretation: proj_u(v) is the unique vector in the direction of u that is closest to v. 'Closest' here means that the residual v - proj_u(v) is perpendicular to u — it has zero component in the direction of u. This perpendicularity of the residual is not an arbitrary choice; it is the DEFINING property of orthogonal projection, and it is what makes projection the best possible approximation of v in the given direction.

toyExample.description: Five 2D data points are projected onto the direction u = [1/√2, 1/√2] (the 45° diagonal direction). We compute each projection length, the projection vector, and the residual vector.

steps.action: Define the projection direction as the unit vector u = [1/√2, 1/√2] ≈ [0.707, 0.707]. Define 5 data points: p₁=[1,2], p₂=[3,1], p₃=[2,3], p₄=[0,2], p₅=[3,3].
steps.result: u is a unit vector (||u|| = √(0.5+0.5) = 1 ✓) pointing at 45° from the horizontal.
steps.insight: We chose this direction to make the computation illustrative. In PCA, the direction would be chosen automatically as the eigenvector of the covariance matrix with the largest eigenvalue.

steps.result: Projection lengths: [2.12, 2.83, 3.54, 1.41, 4.24]. These are the 'coordinates' of each data point along the 45° direction.
steps.insight: The projection length is a single number for each data point — its coordinate along u. Notice that p₅=[3,3] has the largest projection because it is furthest 'along' the 45° direction. p₄=[0,2] has the smallest because it has little x-component.

steps.action: Compute projection VECTORS for each point: proj = (pᵢ·u) × u. For p₁: 2.12 × [0.707, 0.707] = [1.5, 1.5]. For p₃: 3.54 × [0.707, 0.707] = [2.5, 2.5].
steps.result: Projection vectors for p₁ and p₃: [1.5, 1.5] and [2.5, 2.5]. These lie exactly on the 45° line through the origin.
steps.insight: Projection vectors always lie on the line defined by u. They are the 'shadows' of the original points onto the direction u. Notice they differ only in length (the projection scalar), not in direction.

steps.action: Compute residuals for p₁ and p₃: residual = original point - projection vector. r₁ = [1,2] - [1.5,1.5] = [-0.5, 0.5]. r₃ = [2,3] - [2.5,2.5] = [-0.5, 0.5].
steps.result: r₁ = [-0.5, 0.5], r₃ = [-0.5, 0.5]. Both residuals are identical!
steps.insight: p₁ and p₃ have the same residual even though they are different points. This means they differ ONLY in their projection along u — they lie on a line perpendicular to u. Also note: r·u = (-0.5)(0.707) + (0.5)(0.707) = 0 ✓ — the residual is perpendicular to u, as required.

steps.action: Compute the fraction of variance explained by u. Total variance = (1/5)(||p₁||²+...+||p₅||²) after centering. Mean = [9/5, 11/5] = [1.8, 2.2]. Variance explained = mean of squared projection lengths minus mean projection² = Var(projections). Alternatively: variance of {2.12, 2.83, 3.54, 1.41, 4.24} = 0.97.
steps.result: Total variance = Var(X) + Var(Y) ≈ 1.36 + 0.56 = 1.92. Variance explained by u ≈ 0.97. Fraction ≈ 0.97/1.92 ≈ 50.5%.
steps.insight: The 45° direction u captures about 50% of the total variance. PCA would find the direction that maximises this fraction — which for this dataset would be some other direction.

visual.question: As you rotate the projection direction, for which angle is the projection error (residual) minimised? What does this tell you about where the data 'lies' most compactly?
visual.description: An interactive 2D space with 5 data points and a rotatable unit vector u (controlled by a slider for angle). For each data point, the projection onto u is drawn as a coloured dot on the u-axis, and the residual is drawn as a perpendicular dashed line from the data point to its projection. The variance of projections (explained) and the mean squared residual length (unexplained) are shown as bar charts. A second panel plots the variance explained as a function of the projection angle, with a clear peak at the optimal direction (PC1).

technical.text: Orthogonal projection is formally defined by the orthogonality condition: proj_u(v) is the unique vector in span(u) such that v - proj_u(v) ⊥ u. 'Span(u)' means all scalar multiples of u — the line through the origin in direction u. This orthogonality condition uniquely determines the projection formula: proj_u(v) = (v·u / ||u||²) u. For a unit vector û (||û|| = 1), this simplifies to proj_û(v) = (v·û) û. The projection length (scalar projection) is v·û — the dot product. The projection vector is (v·û) û — the length times the direction. The residual is r = v - proj_u(v), and by construction r·u = 0. The squared projection length (v·û)² is the contribution of this data point to the variance in direction û. Summing squared projection lengths over all data points and dividing by n gives the variance of the dataset in direction û — the quantity that PCA maximises.

definitions.definition: proj_u(v) = (v·u / ||u||²) u. The vector in the direction of u that is closest to v. For unit u: proj_û(v) = (v·û) û.
The signed length of the projection: v·û = ||v|| cos(θ), where θ is the angle between v and u. Positive if v and u point in the same hemisphere; negative if opposite.
r = v - proj_u(v). The component of v perpendicular to u. Always satisfies r·u = 0. Represents the information about v that is NOT captured by its projection onto u.
P_u = û ûᵀ (for a unit vector û). A square matrix such that P_u v = proj_û(v) for any vector v. P_u² = P_u (idempotent): projecting twice gives the same result as projecting once.
For a centered dataset X and a unit direction vector û, the variance in direction û is (1/n)||Xû||² = ûᵀ C û, where C is the covariance matrix. This is the quantity PCA maximises.

mathematics.label: Orthogonal projection formula

symbols.proj_u(v): the orthogonal projection of v onto the direction u — a vector in the direction of u
symbols.v · u: dot product of v and u — measures how much v 'aligns' with u
symbols.||u||²: the squared norm of u — normalisation factor that makes the result independent of u's scale
symbols.(v·u / ||u||²): the scalar projection length — how many 'u-lengths' of projection there are
symbols.(v·u / ||u||²) × u: the projection vector — the scalar times the direction vector u

mathematics.plainExplanation: The projection formula takes v and finds the unique point on the line through u (and the origin) that is closest to v. The fraction (v·u) / ||u||² tells us 'how many copies of u do we need to travel to get as close to v as possible'. Multiplying this scalar by u gives the actual vector. For a unit vector û, this simplifies to (v·û) û, since ||û||² = 1.
mathematics.whyNeeded: Projection is the mathematical operation that extracts 'how much of v is in direction u'. This is what PCA uses to compute principal component scores (how much of each data point is in each PC direction) and what SAEs use to compute feature activations (how much of each residual stream vector is in each feature direction).
mathematics.ifRemoved: Without projection, we cannot compute principal component scores, cannot define explained variance, and cannot implement reconstruction from compressed representations. Projection is the atomic operation that underlies all linear dimensionality reduction.
mathematics.encourages: Thinking of projection as 'extracting the component of v along u' — a lossless operation for that component, but one that discards all information about v that is perpendicular to u.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That the projected component is meaningful. We can project any vector onto any direction — the geometric operation is always valid. Whether the resulting projection corresponds to a semantically meaningful quantity depends on whether the projection direction u itself is meaningful.

mathematics.label: Projection residual (orthogonal complement)

symbols.r: the residual vector — the part of v that is NOT captured by the projection onto u
symbols.proj_u(v): the projection of v onto u
symbols.v - proj_u(v): the difference — what remains after removing the u-component from v
symbols.r · u = 0: the orthogonality condition — the residual is always perpendicular to the projection direction

mathematics.plainExplanation: After projecting v onto u, the residual r is everything in v that is perpendicular to u. The fact that r·u = 0 is not just a property — it is the DEFINITION of orthogonal projection: we chose the projection so that the residual would be perpendicular. This perpendicularity is what makes orthogonal projection the best approximation of v in the direction u — any non-orthogonal 'projection' would leave a residual with a u-component, meaning we could do better.
mathematics.whyNeeded: The residual measures what is LOST when we compress v down to its u-component. In PCA, the sum of squared residuals (summed over all data points and all dropped dimensions) equals the reconstruction error. In SAEs, the residual after decoding measures what information the SAE's feature set failed to capture.
mathematics.ifRemoved: Without the residual, we cannot measure reconstruction error, cannot compute explained variance (which requires comparing the projection variance to the total variance), and cannot implement iterative methods that subtract one projection and look for the next.
mathematics.encourages: Always thinking about what is LOST in compression, not just what is retained. The residual is the discarded information — and in SAE research, large residuals are a key diagnostic of inadequate feature coverage.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That a small residual implies the projection is meaningful. The residual can be small because the direction u captures a lot of irrelevant noise variation — not because u captures semantically important structure.

mathematics.label: Variance of data in direction û

symbols.Var_û(X): the variance of the dataset X when projected onto direction û — the quantity PCA maximises
symbols.C: the d×d covariance matrix of the centered data
symbols.ûᵀCû: the quadratic form — computes the variance in direction û from the covariance matrix
symbols.Xû: an n-dimensional vector of scalar projections of all n data points onto û
symbols.(1/n)||Xû||²: the mean squared projection length — equivalent to the variance of the projections

mathematics.plainExplanation: The variance of a dataset in a direction û is computed in two equivalent ways: (1) project all data points onto û, get a list of n numbers, and compute the variance of that list; or (2) compute ûᵀCû using the covariance matrix. Both give the same answer. PCA asks: which direction û maximises ûᵀCû? The answer (proven via Lagrange multipliers) is the eigenvector of C with the largest eigenvalue.
mathematics.whyNeeded: This equation connects projection (a geometric operation) to variance (a statistical quantity) and to the covariance matrix (an algebraic object). It is the bridge that makes PCA mathematically tractable: instead of trying all possible directions by brute force, we can find the maximum by solving the eigenvector equation Cû = λû.
mathematics.ifRemoved: Without this connection, PCA would be intractable — finding the direction of maximum variance would require testing all possible directions. The formula ûᵀCû lets us frame it as an optimization problem with a clean algebraic solution.
mathematics.encourages: Thinking of the covariance matrix as a 'variance function' on directions: for any direction û, the covariance matrix tells you the variance of the data in that direction. The maximum of this function is achieved at the first principal component.
mathematics.doesNotGuarantee: That the direction of maximum variance is the most informative or interpretable direction. High variance in a direction means the data spreads a lot — but that spreading might reflect noise, dataset artifacts, or genuine meaningful variation. PCA cannot distinguish these cases.

methodComparison.question: Orthogonal projection vs nearest-neighbour: two ways to 'summarise' a vector by a simpler object?

methods.name: Orthogonal projection onto a subspace
methods.answer: Given a target subspace (e.g., span of k vectors), orthogonal projection finds the point IN the subspace that is closest to v, where distance is measured by Euclidean norm. The residual is the minimum achievable error. This is the foundation of PCA and linear autoencoders.

methods.name: Nearest neighbour in a discrete codebook
methods.answer: Given a finite set of candidate vectors (codebook), nearest-neighbour finds the codebook entry that is closest to v. This is the foundation of vector quantisation and discrete autoencoders (VQ-VAE). Unlike orthogonal projection, there is no guarantee that the nearest codebook entry lies on a subspace — the error is typically larger, but the result is discrete and constrained to a finite vocabulary.

methodComparison.practicalImplication: SAEs use orthogonal projection (the decoder is a linear map from sparse feature activations). VQ-VAEs use nearest-neighbour lookup. The choice affects whether the compressed representation is continuous (projection) or discrete (codebook). SAEs are appropriate when features are expected to combine linearly; VQ-VAEs are appropriate when the natural clusters are discrete.

evidence.claim: The optimal rank-k approximation of a data matrix (in the Frobenius norm sense) is obtained by projecting the data onto the top-k eigenvectors of the covariance matrix.
evidence.label: [Mathematical Result]
evidence.paraphrase: The Eckart-Young theorem states that the best rank-k approximation of a matrix X (in Frobenius norm) is given by the truncated SVD (or equivalently, projection onto the top-k PCA directions). This is a proved result that motivates using PCA for optimal linear compression.

evidence.claim: In SAEs, the projection of the residual stream onto the feature direction vectors (decoder weights) computes the feature activations.
evidence.label: [Course Interpretation]
evidence.paraphrase: The SAE encoder computes a (possibly nonlinear) activation, but the resulting feature activation values correspond to how much of the input residual stream lies in each feature's direction — making the encoder's core operation a form of projection onto learned feature directions.

failureModes.name: Confusing projection onto a subspace with projection onto a vector
failureModes.mechanism: When reducing to k > 1 dimensions, we project onto a k-dimensional subspace (span of k basis vectors), not just a single vector. The formula for subspace projection is the sum of k one-dimensional projections (onto each basis vector independently), but only when the basis is orthonormal.
failureModes.symptom: Adding a second projection direction to a non-orthogonal basis gives the wrong result — the projections overlap and the sum overcounts the shared variance.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Check that your projection basis vectors are mutually orthogonal. If vectors b₁ and b₂ have b₁·b₂ ≠ 0, the formula 'project onto b₁ then add projection onto b₂' is incorrect.
failureModes.mitigation: Always use orthonormal bases for projection (which PCA automatically provides via eigenvectors). If your basis is not orthonormal, apply Gram-Schmidt orthogonalisation first.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: When projecting SetConCA activations onto multiple SAE feature directions, the features must be treated as a basis (not as independent directions) if they are not orthogonal. Many SAE feature directions are approximately orthogonal but not exactly so — verify before summing projections.

failureModes.name: Treating the projection length as the reconstruction
failureModes.mechanism: The scalar projection length (v·û, a single number) is often confused with the projection vector ((v·û)û, a d-dimensional vector). One is a coordinate; the other is the actual approximation of v in the direction u.
failureModes.symptom: Computing 'feature activations' as scalar dot products and then treating those scalars as if they were reconstructed vectors — dimensionality mismatch when fed into downstream components.
failureModes.diagnosticTest: Check that your 'reconstruction' has the same dimensionality as the original input. If you are computing a 1D scalar and claiming to have 'reconstructed' a 768D vector, you have made this mistake.
failureModes.mitigation: Always multiply the scalar projection by the direction vector to get the reconstruction vector: reconstruction = (v·û) × û. The residual v - (v·û)û is then a 768D vector (for example) with the feature's contribution removed.
failureModes.setconcaRelevance: In analysing SetConCA with SAEs, feature activations are scalars (dot products of the residual stream with the feature direction). To reconstruct the contribution of a feature to the activation, you must multiply the scalar by the d-dimensional feature vector.

setconcaConnection.relevance: Every linear interpretability method applied to SetConCA — PCA, ICA, linear probes, SAE feature decomposition — is fundamentally a projection operation. Understanding projection at this level means you understand what these methods compute and what they discard.
setconcaConnection.whichPart: The SAE analysis of SetConCA's residual stream involves projecting the activation vector for each input onto the SAE's feature directions (decoder columns). The scalar projections are the feature activations; the vector projections are the feature contributions to the residual stream.
setconcaConnection.whichDesignChoice: If SetConCA uses attention-based pooling (as opposed to mean pooling), the pooled vector is a weighted sum of member vectors — itself a form of projection onto a learned weighting direction. The attention weights determine which direction in the space of set members is most important for the pooled representation.
setconcaConnection.whichFailureExplained: Large reconstruction residuals when projecting SetConCA activations onto a k-dimensional SAE basis indicate that k features are insufficient to explain the activation — more features are needed, or the features do not span the relevant directions.
setconcaConnection.proposedExperiment: For each layer of SetConCA's set encoder, compute the mean squared projection residual when projecting activations onto the top-k principal components (for k from 1 to d). Plot this as a function of k. The rate of decrease reveals the intrinsic dimensionality of the representations at each layer.
setconcaConnection.alternativeExplanation: Large projection residuals might indicate that SetConCA's representations are genuinely high-dimensional (many independent features), not that the SAE is failing. Distinguishing these cases requires comparing against a noise baseline: how large would residuals be if the activations were white noise?

exercises.prompt: Predict: if v = [3, 0] and u = [1, 1]/√2 (the 45° direction), is the projection of v onto u longer or shorter than ||v||? Compute the projection length and the residual length. What fraction of v's energy is captured by the projection?
exercises.reveal: Projection length = v·û = 3(1/√2) + 0(1/√2) = 3/√2 ≈ 2.12. Residual length: proj vector = 2.12 × [0.707, 0.707] = [1.5, 1.5]. Residual = [3,0] - [1.5,1.5] = [1.5, -1.5]. ||residual|| = √(2.25+2.25) = √4.5 ≈ 2.12. Check: projection² + residual² = 4.5 + 4.5 = 9 = ||v||² ✓ (Pythagoras). Fraction of energy captured: 4.5/9 = 50%. The 45° direction captures exactly half of v's energy, which makes intuitive sense since v points along the x-axis and u is at 45° to it.

exercises.prompt: Construct a 3D unit vector û and a 3D vector v such that: (a) the projection of v onto û has length exactly 2, (b) the residual (the component of v perpendicular to û) has length exactly 3, and (c) verify using Pythagoras: ||v||² = 2² + 3² = 13.
exercises.reveal: One approach: û = [1,0,0] (x-axis). We need projection length 2 (meaning v·û = 2, so v₁ = 2) and residual length 3 (meaning v₂² + v₃² = 9). Simple choice: v₂ = 3, v₃ = 0. So v = [2, 3, 0]. Check: v·û = 2 ✓. Projection vector = 2×[1,0,0] = [2,0,0]. Residual = [0,3,0]. ||residual|| = 3 ✓. ||v||² = 4+9+0 = 13 = 2²+3² ✓.

exercises.prompt: An SAE achieves 5% reconstruction error (mean squared residual / mean squared input = 0.05). A colleague claims 'the SAE captures 95% of the information in the input'. What is wrong with this claim?
exercises.reveal: Two problems: (1) The SAE captures 95% of the L2 ENERGY (squared norm) of the input, not necessarily 95% of the INFORMATION. The 5% of energy that is discarded might contain the most semantically important features — high-energy directions (high-variance directions) are not automatically the most informative ones. (2) 'Information' in an information-theoretic sense (Shannon entropy, mutual information) is a different quantity from L2 reconstruction error. The SAE reconstruction error measures a geometric property (squared distance in activation space), not an information-theoretic one. A system could have 95% reconstruction accuracy while failing to capture any of the features needed for a specific downstream task.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Prove that the orthogonal projection proj_u(v) = (v·u / ||u||²) u minimises the squared distance between v and any vector of the form αu (i.e., it is the closest point on the line span(u) to v). Use the derivative of ||v - αu||² with respect to α.
rubric: Correctly writes the objective: minimise f(α) = ||v - αu||² = (v - αu)·(v - αu) = ||v||² - 2α(v·u) + α²||u||²
Substitutes back to get the projection: proj_u(v) = α* × u = (v·u / ||u||²) u
Notes that d²f/dα² = 2||u||² > 0, confirming this is a minimum (not maximum)
masteryQuestions.goodVersion: Derive the projection formula by minimising the squared distance from v to the line span(u), showing that orthogonal projection is the unique minimiser.

researchWorksheet.claim: The fraction of variance explained by the top-k principal components (k-dimensional projection) is a reliable measure of how much useful information is retained in a k-dimensional representation.
researchWorksheet.requiredAssumptions: That the semantically useful information in the data correlates with the directions of high variance; that the downstream tasks of interest are linearly extractable from the projected representation; that the projection basis (PCA) aligns with the directions that matter for the task.
researchWorksheet.evidenceProvided: PCA is often able to retain high performance on downstream tasks (e.g., classification) while discarding the bottom principal components, suggesting that variance-captured dimensions contain task-relevant information — in the evaluated settings of specific datasets and tasks.
researchWorksheet.strongestAlternativeExplanation: Task-relevant information might be concentrated in LOW-variance directions that PCA discards. For example, adversarial examples are small-magnitude perturbations (low-variance directions that cause misclassification), suggesting that some semantically critical information lives in low-variance directions that PCA drops.
researchWorksheet.missingControl: Comparing PCA projection to a random projection baseline of the same dimensionality, to assess how much of the task performance comes from the specific choice of PCA vs the mere fact of being in a lower-dimensional space.
researchWorksheet.closestBaseline: A random k-dimensional projection (Gaussian random matrix compressed to k dimensions) on the same downstream task, compared to the k-dimensional PCA projection.
researchWorksheet.falsifyingResult: If a random k-dimensional projection achieved the same downstream task performance as the PCA k-dimensional projection, the claim that PCA's choice of projection directions matters would be substantially weakened.
researchWorksheet.setconcaImplication: When evaluating SetConCA's representations by projecting onto k dimensions and measuring downstream task performance, we should not assume that PCA projection is optimal. The most set-discriminating information might live in directions that are not high-variance — particularly if the training data has many sets that are similar on the high-variance dimensions but different on the low-variance ones.
researchWorksheet.proposedExperiment: For SetConCA, compare downstream task performance when using: (a) top-k PCA projection, (b) random k-dimensional projection, and (c) task-supervised k-dimensional projection (linear probe trained for the downstream task). If (a) and (b) perform similarly but (c) is much better, the variance-relevant directions are not the task-relevant directions.

bridgeToNext.text: We now have the complete toolkit for projection: we can project any data point onto any direction, compute the residual, and measure how much variance a direction explains. The remaining question is: how do we FIND the directions of maximum variance? We cannot try all possible directions by brute force. The answer is eigenvectors. An eigenvector of the covariance matrix is a direction that has a special self-consistency property: when the covariance matrix acts on it (multiplies it), the vector only stretches — it does not rotate. The amount of stretch is the eigenvalue, which turns out to equal the variance of the data in that eigenvector direction. The next lesson builds this concept from scratch.
